name: Summer
description: The summer season — solstice, holidays, weather records, and warm-weather traditions

questions.q: What astronomical event marks the start of summer in the Northern Hemisphere?

source.notes: The Sun reaches its highest point above the horizon.

questions.q: In the Northern Hemisphere, meteorological summer covers which three calendar months?

source.notes: Astronomical summer runs from the June solstice to the September equinox.

questions.q: Which U.S. holiday is celebrated on July 4?

source.notes: Marks the adoption of the Declaration of Independence in 1776.

questions.q: Which Canadian national holiday falls on July 1?

source.notes: Marks the 1867 unification into the Dominion of Canada.

questions.q: Around what date does the summer solstice typically fall in the Northern Hemisphere?

source.notes: Usually June 20-22; the longest day of the year.

questions.q: Which U.S. holiday in late May is considered the unofficial start of summer?

source.notes: Last Monday of May; honors fallen U.S. service members.

questions.q: In which hemisphere does summer run from December to February?

source.notes: Applies to Australia, New Zealand, and southern South America.

questions.q: What is the term, rooted in Roman astronomy, for the hottest stretch of summer?

source.notes: Named for the morning rising of Sirius ('the Dog Star').

questions.q: Bastille Day, the French national day, falls on which summer date?

source.notes: Marks the 1789 storming of the Bastille prison.

questions.q: Which U.S. holiday in early September is considered the unofficial end of summer?

source.notes: First Monday of September; a federal holiday since 1894.

questions.q: Which international multisport event is held every four years during the summer?

source.notes: Modern Olympics founded by Pierre de Coubertin in 1896.

questions.q: Which U.S. national park holds the record for the hottest air temperature ever reliably recorded?

source.notes: 134°F (56.7°C) recorded in 1913 at Furnace Creek.

questions.q: Which 1995 U.S. city heatwave killed more than 700 people in five days?

source.notes: Particularly devastating to elderly residents without air conditioning.

questions.q: In Sweden, which summer solstice festival is one of the most important holidays of the year?

source.notes: Celebrated with maypoles, flower crowns, and dancing.

questions.q: In which year did a European heatwave cause an estimated 70,000+ excess deaths across the continent?

source.notes: audit-corrected 2026-05-09: removed tautological '2003' from the stem. Hit France, Italy, Spain, and Portugal particularly hard.

questions.q: What is the recommended minimum sunscreen SPF rating for general summer use, according to dermatology guidelines?

source.notes: American Academy of Dermatology recommendation.

questions.q: Which New York beach destination is famous for its summer boardwalk and hot-dog eating contest?

source.notes: Home to Nathan's Famous Hot Dog Eating Contest every July 4.

questions.q: Which season is traditionally the peak Hollywood studio release window for tentpole event films?

source.notes: Tradition launched by Jaws (1975) and Star Wars (1977).

questions.q: The highest officially recorded air temperature ever, set in Death Valley in 1913, was what in Fahrenheit?

source.notes: July 10, 1913 at Greenland Ranch (now Furnace Creek).

questions.q: In 2010, a severe heatwave killed an estimated 50,000+ people in which country?

source.notes: Compounded by massive wildfires across western Russia.

questions.q: During the Dust Bowl of the 1930s, which U.S. state was the epicenter of the drought and dust storms?

source.notes: The 'Okies' fled for California in huge numbers.

questions.q: Which Australian town famously recorded 160 consecutive days over 100°F (37.8°C) in the summer of 1923-24?

source.notes: Set a record for the longest heatwave in Australia.
